questions.question: Which of the following methods can be used to magnetise a steel needle?
options: Storing it in a very cold, but dry environment for a long time
Stroking it with one pole of a magnet in one direction
Placing it beside a magnet, and heating both simultaneously
Having it buried beneath the eartth for a few years
questions.explanation: The single-touch stroking method aligns the magnetic domains in the steel needle, turning it into a magnet. Heating actually demagnetises, and the other options have no magnetising effect.

questions.question: Which material is used for making permanent magnets and why?
questions.explanation: Steel retains magnetism for a long time after the magnetising force is removed (high retentivity), making it ideal for permanent magnets. Soft iron has low retentivity and is used for temporary magnets like electromagnets.

questions.question: Which material is preferred for the core of an electromagnet?
questions.explanation: Soft iron magnetises quickly and strongly when current flows but loses its magnetism almost completely when the current stops (low retentivity). This makes it ideal for electromagnets where on/off control is needed.

questions.question: Which of the following will NOT demagnetise a magnet?
options: Passing alternating current through a coil around it
Placing it in contact with soft iron
questions.explanation: Heating, hammering, and AC current all disrupt the alignment of magnetic domains, causing demagnetisation. Placing a magnet in contact with soft iron does not demagnetise it — soft iron may become temporarily magnetised by induction instead.

questions.question: When a piece of soft iron is placed near a permanent magnet, it becomes magnetised. This is an example of:
questions.explanation: Magnetic induction is the process where a magnetic material becomes a magnet when placed in the magnetic field of a permanent magnet, without any physical contact between them.

questions.question: The stroking method of magnetisation using two magnets moving in opposite directions from the centre outward is called:
questions.explanation: In the double-touch method, two opposite poles of two magnets are placed at the centre of the bar and moved outward in opposite directions. This produces a stronger and more uniform magnet than the single-touch method.

questions.question: Which method produces the strongest and most uniform magnet?
questions.explanation: Placing the material inside a solenoid and passing direct current through it produces the strongest and most uniform magnetisation because the magnetic field inside a solenoid is uniform and strong.

questions.question: A magnet is heated strongly and allowed to cool. What happens to its magnetism?
questions.explanation: Heating disrupts the orderly alignment of magnetic domains. Thermal energy causes the domains to randomise, weakening or completely destroying the magnetism.

questions.question: Which of the following is a temporary magnet?
questions.explanation: An electromagnet is a temporary magnet — it exhibits magnetism only when current flows through its coil. Steel bar magnets, compass needles, and lodestone are all permanent magnets.

questions.question: What happens to the magnetic domains inside a material when it becomes magnetised?
options: They align predominantly in one direction
They move to the surface of the material
questions.explanation: In an unmagnetised material, domains are randomly oriented, cancelling each other out. Magnetisation causes the domains to align in the same direction, producing a net magnetic field.

questions.question: Which of the following is an effective way to demagnetise a magnet using electricity?
options: Subjecting the material to a constant unidirectional flow of direct electric current
Passing an alternating current through a coil while slowly withdrawing the magnet
Connecting the permanent magnet directly to the terminals of a chemical battery
Wrapping the magnet with a simple wire carrying a steady, non-fluctuating current
questions.explanation: AC current repeatedly reverses the magnetic field direction, disrupting domain alignment. Gradually withdrawing the magnet reduces the field strength slowly, leaving domains randomised. This is the standard electrical demagnetisation method.

questions.question: A bar of soft iron and a bar of steel are placed in the same magnetising field. After the field is removed:
questions.explanation: Steel has high retentivity — it retains a large portion of its magnetism after the magnetising field is removed. Soft iron has low retentivity and loses most of its magnetism quickly.

scoreMessages.90: Excellent! You have mastered the methods of magnetisation and demagnetisation!
scoreMessages.70: Good work! Review the differences between soft iron and steel, and the electrical method of magnetisation.
scoreMessages.50: Keep practicing! Focus on magnetic domains, retentivity, and the methods used to magnetise and demagnetise materials.
scoreMessages.0: Review the basics: stroking methods, electrical magnetisation, magnetic induction, temporary vs permanent magnets, and demagnetisation techniques.
